s: The Sumerian goddess Inanna descended to the underworld and had to remove one piece of clothing at each of seven gates until she arrived naked and powerless.
q: How many gates did Inanna pass through on her descent to the underworld?
e: This 4,000-year-old myth is the oldest known descent-to-the-underworld story and directly influenced later myths like Persephone's and Orpheus's journeys to the land of the dead.

s: In Chinese mythology, the Monkey King Sun Wukong was born from a magic stone, learned immortality, and caused so much chaos that Buddha trapped him under a mountain for 500 years.
q: Who trapped the Monkey King Sun Wukong under a mountain for 500 years?
e: Sun Wukong is one of literature's most overpowered characters — he defeated heaven's entire army, erased his name from the Book of Death, and only Buddha himself could stop him.

s: The original Cinderella story from 9th-century China features a girl named Ye Xian whose magic fish grants her wishes — predating the European version by 800 years.
q: The earliest known Cinderella story comes from which country?
e: Most people think Cinderella is a European fairy tale, but the earliest known version comes from Tang Dynasty China, showing how universal the rags-to-riches story truly is.

s: In Arthurian legend, the sword Excalibur and the Sword in the Stone are actually two different swords — Excalibur was given to Arthur by the Lady of the Lake.
q: Who gave King Arthur the sword Excalibur?
e: Most people conflate the two swords, but in the original legends, Arthur broke the Sword in the Stone in combat, and Merlin took him to receive Excalibur from the Lady of the Lake as a replacement.

s: Coyote is the most widespread trickster figure in Native American mythology, appearing in stories from over 60 different tribal traditions across North America.
q: Which trickster figure appears in the mythology of over 60 Native American tribal traditions?
e: Unlike most mythological figures who belong to one culture, Coyote appears independently across dozens of Native American nations — always clever, always causing trouble, always teaching lessons.

s: The Greek titan Prometheus stole fire from the gods and gave it to humanity, and as punishment Zeus chained him to a rock where an eagle ate his liver daily — which regrew each night.
q: What punishment did Zeus inflict on Prometheus for stealing fire?
a: An eagle ate his liver daily, which regrew each night
d: He was imprisoned in a cave
He was made mortal and exiled
He had to serve Apollo for a year
He was forced to serve humans
He was cursed with eternal pain
e: Prometheus endured infinite torture for giving humans technology — his story is the original tale of paying an eternal price for progress and has inspired art for millennia.

s: In Egyptian mythology, a person's heart was weighed against the feather of Ma'at after death — if heavier (burdened by sin), a monster called Ammit devoured it.
q: In Egyptian mythology, what was a person's heart weighed against after death?
e: The ancient Egyptians believed your heart literally carried the weight of your deeds. The scariest part? Ammit had the head of a crocodile, body of a lion, and hindquarters of a hippo.

s: Norse mythology predicts the end of the world in Ragnarök, where most gods die — but two humans survive hidden inside the World Tree to repopulate the earth.
q: In Norse mythology, what is the prophesied end of the world called?
e: Unlike most apocalypse myths, Ragnarök includes detailed aftermath — the world is reborn from the sea, and two human survivors (Líf and Lífthrasir) restart civilization.

s: The Japanese yokai called Kappa are water creatures obsessed with politeness — if you bow to a Kappa, it must bow back, spilling the water from the dish on its head and losing its power.
q: How can you defeat a Kappa in Japanese folklore?
a: Bow to it, forcing it to bow back and spill its head-water
e: The Kappa's weakness is literally good manners. Japanese parents used Kappa stories to teach children to be polite AND to stay away from dangerous rivers.

s: The Hindu god Ganesha has an elephant head because his father Shiva, not recognizing his own son, cut off his original head in a rage and replaced it with the first animal head found.
q: Why does the Hindu god Ganesha have an elephant head?
a: Shiva cut off his human head and replaced it with an elephant's
d: His mother Parvati gave him the elephant head
It was a gift from Vishnu
e: This origin story explains one of the world's most recognizable deity forms — Ganesha's elephant head was literally a cosmic accident caused by a family misunderstanding.

s: In Mesopotamian mythology, Gilgamesh — the oldest known literary hero (c. 2100 BCE) — went on a quest for immortality but failed, learning that death gives life its meaning.
q: What is the oldest known work of literary fiction?
e: The Epic of Gilgamesh predates Homer by over 1,000 years and its central message — accept mortality — is still the theme of countless modern stories.

s: The original Brothers Grimm version of Sleeping Beauty didn't end with a kiss — the prince found her already awake, and in earlier versions the story included far darker elements.
q: In the original Brothers Grimm version of Sleeping Beauty, what wakes the princess?
a: She wakes on her own when the enchantment's time expires
e: Disney sanitized fairy tales dramatically. The oldest versions of Sleeping Beauty (like Basile's 1634 version) are shockingly dark by modern standards.

s: Loki in Norse mythology is not actually a god — he is a Jötunn (giant) who was blood-brother to Odin and lived among the Aesir gods.
q: What is Loki's true nature in Norse mythology?
e: Despite being the most famous Norse 'god' of mischief, Loki was technically a giant who talked his way into Asgard — making him the ultimate social infiltrator of mythology.

s: The Aboriginal Australian Rainbow Serpent is one of the oldest continuous religious figures in the world, with depictions dating back at least 6,000 years in rock art.
q: Approximately how old are the oldest known depictions of the Aboriginal Rainbow Serpent?
e: While most mythologies are hundreds or thousands of years old, Aboriginal Australian mythology has been continuously practiced for tens of thousands of years — making the Rainbow Serpent possibly the oldest 'living' deity.

s: In Greek mythology, Hades was not evil — he was actually considered the most fair and just of the three ruling brothers (Zeus, Poseidon, Hades) and simply drew the short straw for the underworld.
q: How did Hades end up ruling the underworld in Greek mythology?
a: He drew lots with his brothers and got the underworld by chance
d: He lost a war with his brothers
He was the youngest and weakest
He angered Zeus and was punished
He was cast out by his siblings
He was born in the underworld
e: Pop culture portrays Hades as a villain, but ancient Greeks saw him as stern but fair. He got the underworld by random lot, not because he was bad — Zeus got the sky and Poseidon the sea the same way.

s: The Norse World Tree Yggdrasil connects nine realms and has a squirrel named Ratatoskr that runs up and down it carrying insults between the eagle at the top and the dragon at the roots.
q: What is the name of the squirrel that runs up and down the Norse World Tree?
e: In a mythology full of epic battles and world-ending prophecies, there's a literal gossip squirrel whose entire job is to make an eagle and a dragon hate each other more.

s: Tanuki in Japanese folklore are shapeshifting raccoon dogs famous for using their magically expandable scrotums as drums, boats, umbrellas, and even disguises.
q: What are tanuki famous for magically expanding in Japanese folklore?
a: Their scrotums, used for various tools and disguises
e: The tanuki is one of folklore's most hilariously absurd creatures — Japanese art frequently depicts them stretching certain anatomy to ridiculous proportions for practical everyday uses.

s: In Hindu mythology, Vishnu has appeared on Earth in 10 avatars (Dashavatara), including a fish, a turtle, a boar, and — according to many traditions — the Buddha himself.
q: How many avatars of Vishnu are there in the Dashavatara?
e: The Dashavatara spans the entire evolution of life: fish, turtle, boar, half-man, dwarf, warrior, prince (Rama), Krishna, Buddha, and Kalki (yet to come). It mirrors biological evolution remarkably.

s: The Slavic god Veles ruled the underworld and was the patron of cattle, magic, and musicians — his eternal conflict with the thunder god Perun formed the core of Slavic mythology.
q: Which Slavic god was the eternal rival of the thunder god Perun?
e: The Perun-Veles conflict mirrors the universal storm-god vs. serpent pattern found in nearly every Indo-European mythology, from Thor vs. Jörmungandr to Indra vs. Vritra.

s: The Greek hero Odysseus spent 10 years fighting at Troy and then 10 more years trying to get home — delayed by a cyclops, sirens, a witch, and his crew eating sacred cattle.
q: How many total years was Odysseus away from home (Troy + journey back)?
e: The Odyssey is the original road trip from hell — everything that could go wrong did, including his crew opening a bag of winds that blew them back to where they started.

s: In African Yoruba mythology, Eshu (or Elegba) is a trickster god who once started a war between two friends by wearing a hat that was red on one side and blue on the other as he walked between them.
q: How did the Yoruba trickster Eshu start a war between two friends?
a: Wore a two-colored hat so each saw a different color
d: Wore a necklace they both saw differently
Spoke a lie that became truth
Danced between them to confuse them
e: Each friend insisted the stranger wore a different colored hat, and their argument escalated into violence — Eshu's lesson was that perspective determines truth, told in the most trollish way possible.

s: Medusa was not always a monster — she was originally a beautiful priestess of Athena who was cursed with snake hair after Poseidon assaulted her in Athena's temple.
q: Why was Medusa cursed with snake hair according to Ovid's version?
a: Athena punished her after Poseidon assaulted her in Athena's temple
d: She was born a monster as punishment
Poseidon turned her into a monster in anger
Zeus cursed her for her beauty
e: The Medusa origin story is one of mythology's great injustices — she was punished for being a victim, which ancient writers like Ovid used as commentary on divine cruelty.

s: In Polynesian mythology, the sky and earth were once locked in an eternal embrace — the god Tāne had to physically push his parents apart to create space for life.
q: In Polynesian creation mythology, who separated the Sky Father and Earth Mother?
e: The Polynesian creation myth is remarkably intimate — the separation of Ranginui (Sky Father) and Papatūānuku (Earth Mother) caused them to weep, creating rain and mist.

s: The Aztec god Quetzalcoatl (Feathered Serpent) was tricked by his rival Tezcatlipoca into getting drunk and committing shameful acts, causing him to exile himself on a raft of serpents.
q: Who tricked Quetzalcoatl into exile in Aztec mythology?
e: Even supreme gods aren't immune to manipulation in Mesoamerican myth — Quetzalcoatl's fall from grace mirrors human struggles with temptation and shame.

s: In Norse mythology, Thor once dressed as a bride — complete with veil and jewelry — to trick a giant who had stolen his hammer Mjölnir.
q: Why did Thor dress as a bride in Norse mythology?
a: To recover his stolen hammer Mjölnir from a giant
d: To escape from a giant woman
e: The mightiest of the Norse gods had to wear a wedding dress and pretend to be the goddess Freyja. When the hammer was placed in the 'bride's' lap, Thor grabbed it and destroyed everyone.

s: The Chinese goddess Nüwa created humanity by molding figures from yellow mud — when she got tired, she dipped a rope in mud and flung it, creating common people from the drops.
q: How did the Chinese goddess Nüwa create the first humans?
a: She molded them from yellow mud
d: She carved them from jade stone
She shaped them from golden sand
She molded them from clay gifted by the gods
She wove them from silk threads
She formed them from dragon scales
She sculpted them from stone quarried from sacred mountains
e: This creation myth has a fascinating class element — the hand-crafted figures became nobles and the splattered drops became commoners, reflecting ancient Chinese social hierarchy.

s: Cerberus, the three-headed dog guarding the Greek underworld, was once defeated by Heracles who wrestled it into submission with his bare hands as his 12th labor.
q: What was Heracles' 12th and final labor?
a: Capture Cerberus, the three-headed guard dog of the underworld
d: Steal the Golden Apples of the Hesperides
Obtain the girdle of Hippolyta, queen of the Amazons
Capture the golden hind of Artemis
Clean the Augean stables in a single day
e: The final and most terrifying of the 12 labors required Heracles to go to the underworld and back — Hades actually gave permission, on the condition Heracles use no weapons.

s: The Celtic goddess Brigid was so important that when Christianity arrived in Ireland, she was simply rebranded as Saint Brigid rather than removed from worship.
q: What happened to the Celtic goddess Brigid when Christianity arrived in Ireland?
a: She was converted into a Christian saint
d: She was completely erased from worship
She was transformed into a mortal priestess
She was imprisoned in a sacred temple
She merged with a Roman goddess instead
She was banished to the Otherworld
She was split into multiple lesser deities
e: Brigid's transition from pagan goddess to Christian saint is one of the clearest examples of religious syncretism — her sacred flame at Kildare was tended by nuns for over 1,000 years.

s: In Japanese mythology, the sun goddess Amaterasu once hid in a cave, plunging the world into darkness — the other gods had to lure her out with a raucous party and a mirror.
q: What lured the Japanese sun goddess Amaterasu out of her cave?
a: A party with wild dancing, laughter, and a mirror
d: A sacred bell that rang continuously
A magical flame that burned eternal
A cosmic trumpet blast heard across realms
The tears of a deity mixed with nectar
A mysterious song sung by celestial beings
e: The gods threw the world's first party to coax the sun back: one goddess danced so wildly the others roared with laughter, and Amaterasu's curiosity drew her out to peek.

s: The Navajo creation myth describes four previous worlds below this one, each destroyed when its inhabitants behaved badly — humanity climbed up through each like ascending through floors.
q: According to Navajo creation mythology, which world do humans currently inhabit?
e: The Navajo view of creation is vertical rather than linear — we're living in the Fifth World, and each previous world had its own color, inhabitants, and moral failing that destroyed it.

s: Fenrir, the giant wolf of Norse mythology, was so powerful that when the gods tried to bind him, he broke every chain — until dwarves made a ribbon from impossible things like the sound of a cat's footsteps.
q: What was the magical ribbon Gleipnir made from in Norse mythology?
a: Impossible things like a cat's footsteps and a mountain's roots
d: A chain forged in the furnace of the sun
Strands of a maiden's hair braided with moonbeams
Rope woven from clouds and starlight
The shadow of a flying bird cast in metal
Threads from a god's clothing spun together
Sinews of a primordial giant bound together
e: The magical ribbon Gleipnir was made from six impossible ingredients: a cat's footsteps, a woman's beard, a mountain's roots, a bear's sinews, a fish's breath, and a bird's spit.

s: The ancient Greek philosopher Euhemerus proposed that all gods were originally real humans whose deeds became exaggerated into divine myths over generations.
q: What is Euhemerism?
a: The theory that gods were originally real humans whose stories were mythologized
d: The belief that myths are purely symbolic stories with no historical basis
The theory that all gods share the same origin story
The practice of turning mortals into gods through ritual sacrifice
The idea that gods were alien visitors to ancient cultures
The notion that gods exist in multiple parallel universes
The concept that myths are mathematical equations describing reality
e: This idea — called Euhemerism — remains influential today and may explain how historical figures like Gilgamesh or the original 'King Arthur' became legendary.

s: In Egyptian mythology, the sun god Ra sailed through the underworld every night in a boat, fighting the chaos serpent Apophis — every sunrise meant Ra had won again.
q: What did the Egyptian sun god Ra do every night according to mythology?
a: Sailed through the underworld fighting the chaos serpent Apophis
d: Stood guard at the gates of the heavens
Collected the souls of the dead in his boat
Mediated disputes between the living and dead
Prepared offerings for the celestial beings above
Sealed the underworld to prevent mortals' entry
Guided new souls through judgment ceremonies
e: Ancient Egyptians saw every single sunrise as evidence of a cosmic battle being won — making dawn the most spiritually significant moment of each day.

s: The Leshy is a Slavic forest spirit that can change its size from as tall as the trees to as small as a blade of grass, and it leads travelers astray by mimicking familiar voices.
q: What is the Leshy in Slavic folklore?
a: A shapeshifting forest spirit that leads travelers astray
d: A water spirit that dwells in lakes and rivers
A mountain guardian that protects sacred peaks
A storm demon that brings lightning and thunder
A trickster fox that deceives travelers
A sea creature that lures sailors to their doom
An underground dweller that guards buried treasures
e: The Leshy explains why people get lost in forests — it was easier to blame a shapeshifting forest lord than admit you can't navigate. Peasants would wear clothes inside-out to confuse it.

s: The Minotaur's labyrinth was designed by Daedalus, the same inventor who later built wax wings for himself and his son Icarus — who flew too close to the sun.
q: Who designed both the Labyrinth and the wax wings in Greek mythology?
e: Daedalus is mythology's greatest engineer: he built an inescapable maze AND invented human flight, but his greatest creation led to his son's death, making it a parable about hubris.

s: In Inuit mythology, Sedna is a sea goddess whose fingers were chopped off by her father — each severed finger became a different sea creature (seals, walruses, whales).
q: What happened to Sedna's severed fingers in Inuit mythology?
a: They became sea creatures (seals, walruses, whales)
d: They became islands scattered across the sea
They transformed into different sea monsters
They became the stars in the night sky
e: Sedna's severed fingers becoming marine animals is one of mythology's most visceral creation stories — Inuit hunters had to keep Sedna happy or she would withhold the sea animals they depended on.

s: In Korean mythology, the founder of Korea (Dangun) was born when a bear spent 100 days in a cave eating only garlic and mugwort to transform into a woman, then married a god.
q: In Korean mythology, what animal transformed into a woman to become the mother of Dangun?
e: This foundation myth explains why bears are sacred in Korean culture — one earned humanity through incredible discipline, while a tiger who attempted the same challenge gave up.

s: Lugh, the Celtic god of skill, was master of every art and craft — he gained entry to the court of the gods by being the only one who could do ALL skills combined.
q: How did the Celtic god Lugh gain entry to the court of the gods?
a: By being the only one who mastered all skills combined
d: By defeating all the other gods in combat
By creating the most powerful magical artifact
By solving a riddle no one else could solve
By seducing the king of the gods
By retrieving a sacred relic from the dead
e: Each god at the court was a specialist, but Lugh was the ultimate generalist. When asked 'Can you do X?' he kept saying yes until they realized no one else could claim every skill.

s: The Jade Emperor in Chinese mythology was originally a mortal who cultivated his virtue for 226.8 million years before achieving supreme divinity.
q: How long did the Jade Emperor cultivate his virtue before becoming supreme god?
e: The sheer timescale dwarfs every other mythology — while Greek gods were born divine and Norse gods live thousands of years, the Jade Emperor earned his position over hundreds of millions of years.

s: The original Little Red Riding Hood (by Perrault, 1697) had no woodsman rescue — the wolf simply ate the girl, and the story ended with a moral warning about talking to strangers.
q: What happens at the end of the original 1697 Little Red Riding Hood by Perrault?
a: The wolf eats the girl and the story ends
d: The huntsman arrives and saves her
She befriends the wolf and returns home
She escapes by outwitting the wolf
She is rescued by her mother
She transforms into a wild animal
The grandma saves her at the last moment
e: The happy ending with the huntsman was added by the Brothers Grimm over 100 years later. Perrault's version was deliberately horrifying to scare children into obedience.

s: Tsukuyomi, the Japanese moon god, was permanently banished from the presence of his sister Amaterasu (the sun) because he killed the food goddess — which is why the sun and moon are never in the sky together.
q: Why are the sun and moon separated in Japanese mythology?
a: The moon god killed a goddess, angering the sun goddess
d: They are siblings who chose to stay together forever
The sun goddess was too bright for the moon to bear
They were cursed by a powerful sorcerer
The sun demanded solitude as punishment for ancient betrayal
A demon drove a wedge between the two siblings
They made a pact to share the sky equally
e: This myth elegantly explains why you rarely see the sun and moon simultaneously — they are estranged siblings who refuse to share the sky after an unforgivable act.

s: The Mayan Hero Twins Hunahpu and Xbalanque defeated the Lords of Death by letting themselves be killed, ground into powder, and thrown into a river — then regenerating and returning to trick the death gods.
q: How did the Mayan Hero Twins ultimately defeat the Lords of Death?
a: They tricked the death lords into being killed and didn't resurrect them
d: They defeated them through force and destroyed the underworld
They made a pact with the death lords to share power
They challenged and lost but were allowed to return to life
They freed all the trapped souls and rebuilt the world
They sacrificed themselves to imprison the death lords forever
They won through a magical tournament of games
e: The Hero Twins weaponized resurrection itself. They demonstrated bringing each other back to life as entertainment, then when the death lords wanted the same trick performed on them, the Twins simply didn't bring them back.

s: In Greek mythology, Prometheus stole fire from the gods and gave it to humanity, for which Zeus punished him by chaining him to a rock where an eagle ate his liver daily.
q: In Greek mythology, who stole fire from the gods and gave it to humanity?
e: Prometheus was a Titan who defied Zeus by stealing fire from Mount Olympus. His liver regenerated each night, making his punishment eternal until Heracles freed him.

s: The Norse world tree Yggdrasil connects nine realms including Asgard (realm of the gods), Midgard (realm of humans), and Hel (realm of the dead).
q: What is the name of the great world tree in Norse mythology that connects the nine realms?
e: Yggdrasil is an immense ash tree at the center of Norse cosmology. Its branches and roots connect all nine worlds, and various creatures inhabit it including the dragon Nidhogg and the eagle at its crown.

s: Anubis, the Egyptian god of mummification and the afterlife, has the head of a jackal and guided souls through the underworld to the Hall of Judgment.
q: Which Egyptian god with a jackal head guided souls to the afterlife and oversaw mummification?
e: Anubis was one of the most important gods in Egyptian funerary practices. He oversaw embalming and weighed the hearts of the dead against the feather of Ma'at to determine their fate.

s: In Japanese folklore, the kitsune is a fox spirit that gains additional tails as it grows older and more powerful, with nine tails representing the pinnacle of wisdom and magical ability.
q: In Japanese folklore, what type of spirit gains additional tails as it grows older and more powerful?
e: Kitsune are intelligent fox spirits in Japanese mythology. A young kitsune has one tail, but as it ages over centuries, it can grow up to nine tails. Nine-tailed kitsune are considered nearly divine in power.

s: Hindu mythology describes the Samudra Manthan, or Churning of the Ocean of Milk, where gods and demons cooperated to extract the elixir of immortality (amrita) from the cosmic ocean.
q: What is the Hindu mythological event where gods and demons churned the cosmic ocean to obtain the elixir of immortality?
e: The Samudra Manthan is one of the most famous episodes in Hindu mythology. Mount Mandara was used as the churning rod and the serpent Vasuki as the rope, with devas and asuras pulling from opposite ends.

s: In Norse mythology, Ragnarök is the prophesied apocalypse where most of the gods, including Odin and Thor, will die in a final battle before the world is reborn.
q: What is the Norse prophesied end of the world called, in which most gods perish before the world is reborn?
e: Ragnarök involves a great battle at Vígríðr where gods fight giants and monsters. Odin is swallowed by the wolf Fenrir, Thor kills the Midgard Serpent but dies from its venom, and the world sinks into the sea before rising anew.

s: Medusa was one of three Gorgon sisters in Greek mythology, and she was the only mortal one. Anyone who looked directly at her face was turned to stone.
q: In Greek mythology, which Gorgon sister was mortal and turned anyone who looked at her to stone?
e: Medusa had living venomous snakes in place of hair. The hero Perseus defeated her by using a polished shield as a mirror to avoid her gaze, then beheaded her. From her blood sprang Pegasus, the winged horse.

s: The Hindu god Ganesha has an elephant head because, according to myth, his father Shiva beheaded him not recognizing him and then replaced his head with that of the first creature found — an elephant.
q: According to Hindu mythology, why does the god Ganesha have an elephant head?
a: Shiva beheaded him and replaced his head with an elephant's
d: Parvati gave him the elephant head as a gift
He was born with an elephant's head naturally
He chose it for his superior power
His original head was lost in battle
Brahma gifted it to him during a ceremony
He cursed himself with it as penance
e: Parvati created Ganesha from turmeric paste and set him to guard her door. When Shiva returned and was denied entry, he beheaded the boy in anger. Upon learning this was his son, Shiva replaced the head with an elephant's to revive him.
